narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 54% chance you'll face challenges passing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold for approval. Your profile shows solid but below-average academic standings—you placed in the 4th decile for grades in your third year of school (compared to the typical student's 5.5th decile) and 5th decile in your first year (versus the average 5.7th)—along with an undergraduate GPA of 2.6, well below the usual 3.2. Your LSAT score of 24 is notably lower than the dataset average of nearly 37, your family falls into the middle income bracket (around the typical level), you're an Asian male planning full-time studies, which aligns closely with most applicants. The model's decision was driven primarily by your third-year decile ranking, as being below average there had the strongest pull toward predicting bar struggles. Coming in second was your first-year decile, which similarly signaled lower performance relative to peers and reinforced concerns. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, with its much lower value compared to others pushing the prediction further toward expected failure. Family income and racial background followed closely, each contributing modestly in the same direction, while your gender as male offered a slight counterbalance toward success, and full-time status had minimal impact. Overall, these academic markers set you apart from typical students who tend to have stronger high school-like rankings, higher test scores, and GPAs that correlate with bar success. While demographics like yours are not uncommon, your key metrics fell short of the distributions where most passers cluster. To boost your future odds, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim closer to or above 37 through targeted practice, build undergraduate-level study habits if still in school to lift your GPA toward 3.2 or better, and prioritize rigorous bar prep courses emphasizing the skills reflected in your lower deciles and test score. With dedicated effort in these areas, you could shift your profile toward the stronger performers who pass on the first try. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these gaps—keep pushing forward.
